docs.title: Ember
docs.text: Ember is a lightweight deep learning Python package with C and CUDA implementations, designed for performance and simplicity.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Lightweight: Minimal overhead, focused on core tensor operations.
  • Hardware Accelerated:
  • Pure C implementation for CPU.
  • CUDA implementation for NVIDIA GPUs.
  • NumPy Compatible: Seamless conversion to and from NumPy arrays.
  • Autograd Support: (Upcoming) Automatic differentiation for training neural networks.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import ember
import numpy as np

# Create a tensor from a list
t = ember.Tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])

# Perform operations
t2 = ember.sin(t)
print(t2.to_list())
# Output: [0.84147096, 0.90929741, 0.14112000] (approx)

docs.title: Class ember.Tensor
docs.text: The core data structure of the Ember library. A Tensor represents a multi-dimensional array of elements, similar to NumPy arrays but optimized for Ember's backend (C/CUDA).

docs.title: __init__(data)
docs.text:
Creates a Tensor from a Python list, a scalar, or a NumPy array.

  • Arguments:
  • data (list | number | numpy.ndarray): The data to initialize the tensor with. NumPy arrays are cast to float32.
  • Returns: A new Tensor instance.
import ember
import numpy as np

t = ember.Tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
u = ember.Tensor(np.eye(3, dtype=np.float32))

Note: the backend is currently float32-only. Integer lists are stored, but all computation happens in float32.

docs.title: from_np(array)
docs.text:
Creates a Tensor from a NumPy array.

  • Arguments:
  • array (numpy.ndarray): The NumPy array to convert.
  • Returns: A new Tensor instance.

docs.title: to_np() -> numpy.ndarray
docs.text: Converts the tensor back to a NumPy array.

docs.title: to_list() -> list
docs.text: Converts the tensor data to a (possibly nested) Python list.

docs.title: reshape(new_shape: tuple[int, ...]) -> Tensor
docs.text:
Reshapes the tensor in place and returns it (the same object).

  • Arguments:
  • new_shape: The new shape. One dimension can be -1, which will be inferred.

docs.title: Operators
docs.text:
Tensor objects support standard Python operators.

Operator Method Description Formula + __add__ Elementwise addition \(c_i = a_i + b_i\) - __sub__ Elementwise subtraction \(c_i = a_i - b_i\) * __mul__ Elementwise multiplication \(c_i = a_i \cdot b_i\) / __truediv__ Elementwise division \(c_i = \frac{a_i}{b_i}\) @ __matmul__ Matrix multiplication (2-D only) \(C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}\) ** __pow__ Elementwise power \(c_i = a_i^{b_i}\) - __neg__ Elementwise negation \(b_i = -a_i\) > __gt__ Elementwise greater than \(c_i = 1 \text{ if } a_i > b_i \text{ else } 0\) < __lt__ Elementwise less than \(c_i = 1 \text{ if } a_i < b_i \text{ else } 0\)

The in-place forms +=, -=, *=, /= are also supported and mutate the tensor object in place (optimizers rely on this). Scalar operands and NumPy-style broadcasting (for +, -, *, /) are supported throughout.

docs.title: Functional API
docs.text: All functional operations are available directly under the ember namespace.

docs.title: Elementwise Math Operations
docs.text:
Functions that operate on each element of the input tensor independently. For an input tensor \(A\) with elements \(a_i\), the output tensor \(B\) has elements \(b_i\):

\[b_i = f(a_i)\]

docs.title: Trigonometric
docs.text: Function Description Equation ember.sin(x) Sine \(b_i = \sin(a_i)\) ember.cos(x) Cosine \(b_i = \cos(a_i)\) ember.tan(x) Tangent \(b_i = \tan(a_i)\) ember.ctg(x) Cotangent \(b_i = \cot(a_i)\)

docs.title: Hyperbolic
docs.text: Function Description Equation ember.sinh(x) Hyperbolic Sine \(b_i = \sinh(a_i)\) ember.cosh(x) Hyperbolic Cosine \(b_i = \cosh(a_i)\) ember.tanh(x) Hyperbolic Tangent \(b_i = \tanh(a_i)\) ember.ctgh(x) Hyperbolic Cotangent \(b_i = \coth(a_i)\)

docs.title: Other
docs.text: Function Description Equation ember.exp(x) Exponential \(b_i = e^{a_i}\) ember.log(x) Natural logarithm \(b_i = \ln(a_i)\) ember.sqrt(x) Square root \(b_i = \sqrt{a_i}\)

docs.title: Reductions & Shape
docs.text:
Function Description ember.sum(x) Sum of all elements (returns a float). ember.sum(x, axis=k, keepdims=False) Sum along axis k, returning a Tensor. ember.amax(x, axis=k, keepdims=False) Maximum along axis k (reduction; distinct from the element-wise ember.max). ember.softmax(x, axis=-1) Numerically-stable softmax along axis. ember.T(x) Transpose of a 2-D tensor.

Matrix multiplication (@) supports 2-D operands and batched N-D operands whose leading (batch) dimensions match, e.g. (B, n, k) @ (B, k, m) -> (B, n, m).

docs.title: Binary Elementwise Operations
docs.text:
Functions that extend elementwise operations to two tensors (or a tensor and a scalar). For inputs \(A, B\), the output \(C\) is:

\[c_i = f(a_i, b_i)\] Function Description Equation ember.max(a, b) Elementwise maximum \(c_i = \max(a_i, b_i)\) ember.min(a, b) Elementwise minimum \(c_i = \min(a_i, b_i)\)

docs.title: Random Module (ember.random)
docs.text: Utilities for generating tensors with random or constant values.

docs.title: ember.random.seed(value)
docs.text:
Seeds the random number generator so that uniform (and any layer that uses it, such as weight initialisation and dropout) becomes reproducible.

  • Arguments:
  • value (int): The seed.

docs.title: ember.random.uniform(low, high, size)
docs.text:
Draws samples from a uniform distribution.

  • Arguments:
  • low (float): Lower boundary of the output interval.
  • high (float): Upper boundary of the output interval.
  • size (tuple[int]): Shape of the output tensor.
  • Equation: \(x_i \sim U(\text{low}, \text{high})\)

docs.title: ember.random.constant(value, size)
docs.text:
Return a new tensor of given shape and type, filled with value.

  • Arguments:
  • value (float): Fill value.
  • size (tuple[int]): Shape of the output tensor.
  • Equation: \(x_i = \text{value}\)

docs.title: ember.random.zeros(size)
docs.text: Returns a new tensor of given shape, filled with zeros.

docs.title: ember.random.ones(size)
docs.text: Returns a new tensor of given shape, filled with ones.

docs.title: Neural Networks (ember.nn)
docs.text:
Components for building neural networks. All public classes are available directly under ember.nn, e.g. ember.nn.Linear, ember.nn.ReLU.

Every layer implements a common interface (defined by ember.nn.base.Layer):

  • forward(x, training) -> Tensor and backward(grad_y) -> Tensor
  • parameters() -> list[Tensor] and gradients() -> list[Tensor | None]
  • reset() to (re-)initialise state
  • calling the layer (layer(x)) runs forward and caches inputs/outputs.

docs.title: ember.nn.Linear(in_features, out_features)
docs.text:
Applies a linear transformation to the incoming data.

  • Arguments:
  • in_features (int): Size of each input sample.
  • out_features (int): Size of each output sample.
  • Forward: \(y = x W + b\)
  • Where \(W\) is weight matrix of shape (in_features, out_features) and \(b\) is bias of shape (out_features,).

Methods:

  • reset(): Re-initializes weights and biases.
  • parameters() -> list[Tensor]: Returns [w, b].
  • gradients() -> list[Tensor | None]: Returns [grad_w, grad_b].

docs.title: ember.nn.Dropout(p)
docs.text:
Inverted dropout. During training, zeroes each element with probability p and scales the survivors by \(1/(1-p)\); during inference it is the identity.

  • Arguments:
  • p (float): Drop probability, 0 <= p < 1.

docs.title: ember.nn.Sequential(*layers)
docs.text:
Chains layers together, running them in order on forward and in reverse on backward. parameters()/gradients() aggregate across all child layers.

import ember.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(784, 128),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(128, 10),
)

docs.title: Activations
docs.text: Activation functions applied elementwise.

docs.title: ember.nn.activations.ReLU
docs.text:
Rectified Linear Unit.

  • Equation: \(f(x) = \max(0, x)\)

docs.title: ember.nn.activations.Sigmoid
docs.text:
Sigmoid function.

  • Equation: \(f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}\)

docs.title: ember.nn.activations.Tanh
docs.text:
Hyperbolic Tangent function.

  • Equation: \(f(x) = \tanh(x)\)

docs.title: ember.nn.activations.GELU
docs.text:
Gaussian Error Linear Unit (using tanh approximation).

  • Equation: \(f(x) \approx 0.5 x (1 + \tanh(0.8 x))\)

docs.title: Optimizers (ember.optim)
docs.text:
Optimizers update a list of parameters from a matching list of gradients. All optimizers implement ember.optim.base.Optimizer:

  • __init__(parameters, ...)
  • apply(gradients: list[Tensor]) -> None

docs.title: ember.optim.SGD(parameters, lr=0.01, momentum=0.0)
docs.text: Stochastic gradient descent with optional (Polyak) momentum: v = momentum*v + g, then p -= lr*v. With momentum=0 this is plain gradient descent.

docs.title: ember.optim.Adam(parameters, lr=1e-3, betas=(0.9, 0.999), eps=1e-8)
docs.text:
Adam optimizer.

  • Arguments:
  • parameters (list[Tensor]): Parameters to optimize.
  • lr (float): Learning rate.
  • betas (tuple[float, float]): Coefficients for the running averages of the gradient and its square.
  • eps (float): Term added to the denominator for numerical stability.

docs.title: ember.optim.AdamW(parameters, lr=1e-3, betas=(0.9, 0.999), eps=1e-8, weight_decay=0.01)
docs.text:
Adam with decoupled weight decay (p *= 1 - lr*weight_decay applied directly to the parameters rather than folded into the gradient).

import ember.optim as optim

opt = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
# ... after a forward/backward pass ...
opt.apply(model.gradients())

docs.title: Losses (ember.loss)
docs.text: Losses implement ember.loss.base.Loss: forward(pred, target) -> float (also callable) caches state, and backward() -> Tensor returns the gradient of the loss with respect to pred.

docs.title: ember.loss.MSELoss()
docs.text: Mean squared error over all elements: mean((pred - target) ** 2).

docs.title: ember.loss.CrossEntropyLoss()
docs.text:
Softmax cross-entropy for classification, averaged over the batch. Expects pred to be raw logits of shape (batch, classes) and target to be one-hot probabilities of the same shape. Uses the log-sum-exp trick for stability.

import ember.loss as loss

criterion = loss.CrossEntropyLoss()
value = criterion(logits, one_hot_targets)  # scalar float
grad = criterion.backward()                 # Tensor, dL/dlogits

docs.title: Extending Ember
docs.text: Ember is designed so that the most common extension — adding a new element-wise tensor operator — touches as few places as possible.

docs.title: Architecture at a glance
docs.text:
ember/                     Python front-end
  tensor/tensor.py         Tensor class + functional ops (sin, sum, ...)
  nn/, optim/, random/     layers, optimizers, samplers
  _core/                   compiled extension modules (_tensor, _em_random) + .pyi stubs

src/                       C / CUDA back-end
  core/                    memory + type definitions (CPU and GPU variants)
  tensor/
    operators.def          <-- single source of truth for element-wise ops
    operators.h            declarations   (generated from operators.def)
    operators_cpu.c        CPU kernels    (generated from operators.def)
    operators_gpu.cu       CUDA kernels   (generated from operators.def)
    tensor_api.c           Python bindings (generated from operators.def)
  random/                  distributions (CPU + GPU)

The CPU (*_cpu.c) and GPU (*_gpu.cu) files provide the same functions; CMake compiles exactly one set depending on whether a CUDA toolkit is found, so the rest of the library is backend-agnostic.

docs.title: Adding an element-wise operator
docs.text:
All element-wise operators live in a single table, src/tensor/operators.def. Each line is expanded by X-macros into the C declaration, the CPU implementation, the CUDA kernel, and the Python binding. The expression must be valid in both C and CUDA (fmaxf, powf, expf, … exist in both).

To add, say, an abs unary operator:

  1. Add one line to operators.def:
EMBER_UNARY_OP(abs, fabsf(a[i]))

(Use EMBER_BINARY_OP, EMBER_SCALAR_OP, or EMBER_BROADCAST_OP for the other kinds.) This alone gives you the abs_tensor kernel on both backends and the _abs function on the ember._core._tensor module.

  1. Expose it in Python — add a thin wrapper in ember/tensor/tensor.py:
def abs(a: Tensor) -> Tensor:
    return _unary_op_wrapper(a, "abs()", _abs)

and re-export it from ember/tensor/__init__.py and ember/__init__.py.

  1. Add the type stub in ember/_core/_tensor.pyi:
def _abs(a: _Tensor) -> _Tensor: ...
  1. Add a test in tests/tensor/test_tensor.py (the parametrized suite makes this a one-line addition to the UNARY_OPS list).

Rebuild and test:

uv pip install -e .
uv run --group dev pytest

docs.title: Adding a layer, optimizer, or loss
docs.text: Layers subclass ember.nn.base.Layer and implement forward, backward, parameters, gradients, and reset. Optimizers subclass ember.optim.base.Optimizer and implement apply. Losses subclass ember.loss.base.Loss and implement forward and backward. Because these are pure Python built on Tensor, no C changes are required.

docs.title: Non-element-wise operators
docs.text: Operators that are not purely element-wise (matmul, transpose, sum, sum_axis) are written by hand in operators_cpu.c / operators_gpu.cu and declared in operators.h, then bound in tensor_api.c. Follow the existing functions as templates.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Ember requires Python 3.11 or later. You can install it from source.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• CMake (>= 3.18)
  • C Compiler (GCC, Clang, or MSVC)
  • CUDA Toolkit (Optional, for GPU support)

docs.title: Installing from Source
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/pawel002/ember
cd ember
  1. Create a virtual environment (recommended) and download dependencies:
uv sync
source .venv/bin/activate
  1. Install the package:
uv pip install -e .

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: Ember provides a Tensor class and various mathematical operations.

docs.title: Creating Tensors
docs.text:
You can create tensors from Python lists or NumPy arrays.

import ember as em
import numpy as np

from ember import Tensor

# From list
a = Tensor([1, 2, 3])

# From NumPy
np_arr = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], dtype=np.float32)
b = Tensor(np_arr) # Automatically converts from buffer

docs.title: Operations
docs.text: Ember supports element-wise operations and broadcasting with scalars.

docs.title: Arithmetic
docs.text:
c = a + b
d = a * 2.0
e = em.max(a, b)

docs.title: Trigonometric & Hyperbolic
docs.text:
# Standard trig
s = em.sin(a)
c = em.cos(a)

# Hyperbolic
sh = em.sinh(a)
th = em.tanh(a)

docs.title: Matrix Multiplication
docs.text:
Use the @ operator for 2-D matrix multiplication.

import ember as em
from ember import Tensor

a = Tensor([[1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 4.0]])  # (2, 2)
b = Tensor([[5.0, 6.0], [7.0, 8.0]])  # (2, 2)

c = a @ b            # matrix product
d = em.T(a)          # transpose
s = em.sum(a)        # sum of all elements -> float
row_sums = em.sum(a, axis=1)  # sum along an axis -> Tensor

docs.title: Data Exchange
docs.text:
Convert back to standard Python/NumPy types easily.

# To a (nested) Python list
data_list = c.to_list()

# To NumPy
data_np = c.to_np()

docs.title: Building and Training a Model
docs.text:
Ember ships small nn and optim modules with explicit (manual) gradients.

import numpy as np

import ember as em
import ember.loss as loss
import ember.nn as nn
import ember.optim as optim
from ember import Tensor

em.random.seed(0)  # reproducible weight init

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(4, 8),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(8, 1),
)
opt = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-2)
criterion = loss.MSELoss()

x = Tensor(np.random.randn(16, 4).astype(np.float32))
target = Tensor(np.random.randn(16, 1).astype(np.float32))

# forward + loss
pred = model(x, training=True)
value = criterion(pred, target)

# backward, then an optimizer step
model.backward(criterion.backward())
opt.apply(model.gradients())
